Position the completed pocket in the centre of one outer heart and secure it using ladder stitch around the sides and bottom, leaving the curved upper edge open. Pair each outer heart with a lining heart, right sides together. Mark the template points and stitch only along the upper curved sections between the marked points. Repeat for the remaining set.

tip.text: Accidentally stitching across the top of the pocket will prevent it from functioning as a usable pocket.

steps.title: Heart Bag - Assemble Outer Bag and Lining
steps.content: Turn both heart sections right side out and smooth the curved seams. Place the outer heart sections together with the wadded sides facing each other and stitch around the outer raw edges only. Keep the lining pieces folded away from the stitching. Turn the project so the lining pieces face each other with right sides together. Stitch around the lining edges, leaving a 2.36 inches opening for turning. Turn the entire bag right side out through the gap and shape the curves.

tip.text: Make sure the lining stays clear of the outer seam when sewing or unwanted layers may get caught in the stitching.

steps.title: Heart Bag - Make the Strap and Finish the Bag
steps.content: Close the lining opening and tuck the lining inside the bag. Cut a fabric strip measuring 1.57 x 15.75 inches for the strap. Fold it lengthwise, stitch along the long edge and leave a turning gap approximately 1.97 inches from one end. Move the seam to the centre, stitch both short ends closed and turn the strap right side out. Press and close the gap with ladder stitch. Sew a jump ring to each side of the bag and attach the strap ends. Create a decorative charm using ribbon scraps and pom pom trim, then attach it to a charm clip and secure it to the bag.

tip.text: Press the strap after turning it right side out to create a smoother finish and help it sit neatly on the shoulder.

items.name: City Curve Sling Bag
items.materialNeeded:
Outer fabric fat quarters (2)
Lining fabric fat quarters (2)
Zip (17.32 inches)
Flanged piping (39.37 inches)
Webbing strap
Swivel clip fasteners (2)
D-rings (2)
Tri-glide bar adjuster
Matching sewing thread
items.techniques:
Zip Installation
Piping Insertion
Bag Construction
Lining Construction
Topstitching
Pocket Construction
Strap Construction
Hardware Installation
Hand Sewing
Turning and Pressing

steps.title: Crossbody Bag - Prepare Pattern Pieces and Interior Pocket
steps.content: Download and print the templates. Cut the top, front, back and zip cover pieces from the outer fabric. Cut the pocket, top, front and back pieces from the lining fabric. Press a 0.39 inches hem along both sides of the pocket. Position the pocket centrally on the lining back piece and stitch around three sides, leaving the top edge open. Reinforce the upper corners with extra stitching.

tip.text: Backstitch the pocket opening corners several times because these areas receive the most stress during daily use.

steps.title: Crossbody Bag - Add Fabric Covers to the Zip Ends
steps.content: Trim the zip to 17.32 inches. Press a 0.39 inches hem on one short edge of each zip cover. Place each cover right sides together with a zip end and hand stitch along the fold line. Fold the cover over the zip end and secure the raw edges with a few stitches. Repeat for the opposite end.

tip.text: Check that the zip slider remains accessible before stitching both zip covers permanently.

steps.title: Crossbody Bag - Attach Piping and First Side of the Zip
steps.content: Baste piping along edge A of the outer top piece with the raw edges aligned. With right sides together, pin and baste the piped edge to one side of the zip, making sure the zip is fully closed and the slider is positioned correctly.

tip.text: Stretching the piping while basting can cause ripples and uneven curves around the finished bag.

steps.title: Crossbody Bag - Attach the First Lining Section
steps.content: Position the matching lining top piece on the reverse side of the zip, sandwiching the zip between the outer fabric and lining. Stitch through all layers using a zipper foot. Remove any temporary basting and turn the fabrics right side out.

tip.text: Use a zipper foot to sew closer to the zip teeth and achieve a cleaner professional finish.

steps.title: Crossbody Bag - Attach Front Panel and Remaining Zip Side
steps.content: Baste piping along edge C of the outer front panel. Attach the piped edge to the remaining side of the zip. Add the matching lining front piece to the reverse side and stitch through all layers. Remove basting and turn right side out.

tip.text: Keep the piping cord away from the needle path to avoid breaking needles or creating uneven seams.

steps.title: Crossbody Bag - Create D-Ring Tabs
steps.content: Cut two rectangles measuring 1.57 x 1.97 inches from outer fabric. Fold each piece lengthwise and stitch along the long edge to form tubes. Turn right side out, move the seam to the centre back and fold each tube in half around a D-ring. Baste the raw edges to both ends of the zip section.

tip.text: Position the seam on the inside of the tab so it remains hidden after the D-rings are attached.

steps.title: Crossbody Bag - Assemble the Outer Bag
steps.content: Lay the zipped section flat with the right side facing up and open the zip. Position the outer back piece right sides together on top. Baste around the perimeter, securing the back piece to the outer top and front sections. Stitch along the seam line and remove the basting stitches.

tip.text: Open the zip before sewing the bag closed or turning the bag later will be difficult.

steps.title: Crossbody Bag - Assemble and Close the Lining
steps.content: Place the lining back piece right sides together with the remaining lining sections. Stitch around the perimeter, leaving a 4-5 inches gap along the curved lining edge for turning. Turn the bag right side out through the opening, then close the gap with hand stitching and place the lining inside the bag.

tip.text: Leaving too small a turning gap can make it difficult to pull the curved bag shape through without distortion.

steps.title: Crossbody Bag - Prepare the Adjustable Strap
steps.content: Cut webbing to fit the wearer, adding approximately 3.15 inches to the desired length. Seal or fold the ends. Thread one end through the tri-glide adjuster and stitch it in place. Add one swivel clip to the opposite end.

tip.text: Verify the strap hardware orientation before sewing because twisted webbing is difficult to correct afterward.

steps.title: Crossbody Bag - Attach Hardware and Finish
steps.content: Thread the remaining end of the webbing through the tri-glide adjuster and attach the second swivel clip. Adjust the strap length as desired and clip both ends onto the D-rings attached to the bag to complete the project.

tip.text: Test the strap length while wearing the bag before trimming excess webbing.

items.name: Bloom Embroidered Frame Purse
items.materialNeeded:
Linen fabric
Contrasting silk lining fabric
Lightweight wadding
Felt scraps
Six-strand embroidery floss in assorted colors
Coordinating sewing thread
Crewel needle
Erasable fabric marker
Embroidery hoop
Purse frame
items.techniques:
French Knots
Pistil Stitch
Hand Embroidery
Hand Sewing
Bag Construction
Lining Construction
Boxed Corners
Slip Stitching
Frame Purse Installation
Pressing

steps.title: Prepare the Fabric and Layout
steps.content: Download and print the template. Trace the purse shape onto card and use it to draw the design area onto the linen fabric. Place the fabric into an embroidery hoop, ensuring it is taut and wrinkle-free before beginning the embroidery.

tip.text: Keep the fabric evenly tensioned in the embroidery hoop to produce neater and more consistent embroidery stitches.

steps.title: Attach Felt Circles
steps.content: Cut seven small circles from felt scraps. Arrange them on the linen fabric, keeping them at least 0.31 inches from the purse edge. Secure each circle using small oversewing stitches with a single strand of embroidery thread.

tip.text: Placing felt circles too close to the seam allowance can cause them to become distorted or partially hidden during construction.

steps.title: Stitch French Knot Flower Centers
steps.content: Using three strands of embroidery floss, surround each felt circle with a border of French knots. Change thread colors as desired and add additional rings of French knots around the original circles to build layered floral motifs.

tip.text: Avoid pulling French knots too tightly, as this can distort the linen fabric and create puckering.

steps.title: Add Decorative Embroidery Details
steps.content: Continue filling the surrounding space with scattered French knots and pistil stitches. Use the felt circles as focal points and vary thread colors to create a balanced embroidered design across the purse front.

tip.text: Distribute colors evenly across the design to prevent one area from appearing visually heavier than the others.

steps.title: Cut and Prepare the Purse Pieces
steps.content: Remove the embroidery from the hoop and press the fabric face down on a folded towel. Using the template, cut one embroidered outer front, one outer back, two lining pieces and two wadding pieces. Transfer all template markings, including points A and B.

tip.text: Press embroidered fabric face down on a soft surface to avoid flattening the raised French knots.

steps.title: Assemble the Outer Purse and Lining
steps.content: Tack one piece of wadding to the wrong side of each outer fabric piece. Place the outer pieces right sides together and stitch the side and bottom seams between points A. Repeat the same process with the lining pieces.

tip.text: Failing to align the marked points accurately can affect how the purse fits into the frame later.

steps.title: Create the Boxed Corners
steps.content: Match each side seam with the bottom seam to form a triangle at each lower corner. Measure 1.18 inches from the tip of each triangle and stitch across. Trim away the excess fabric, leaving a small seam allowance. Repeat for both the outer purse and lining.

tip.text: Measure each corner carefully before stitching to keep the purse base symmetrical.

steps.title: Join Outer Purse and Lining
steps.content: Turn the outer purse right side out and place it inside the lining with right sides facing together. Stitch the upper flap sections together, leaving an opening between the marked B points. Turn the purse right side out through the opening and close the gap with slip stitches.

tip.text: Do not make the turning opening too small, or bulky embroidered sections may be difficult to pull through safely.

steps.title: Attach the Purse Frame
steps.content: Push one side of the purse opening into the purse frame channel. Using three strands of embroidery floss, sew through the frame holes and all fabric layers using backstitch. Repeat on the opposite side until the purse frame is fully attached.

tip.text: Attaching one side of the frame without checking alignment first can leave the purse opening uneven.

steps.title: Finish the Project
steps.content: Check that the purse frame opens and closes smoothly. Adjust any loose stitches if necessary and attach an optional wrist strap to complete the purse.

tip.text: Test the frame mechanism several times before trimming thread tails to ensure everything operates correctly.

items.name: Floral Pocket Tote Bag
items.materialNeeded:
Floral print cotton fabric
Geometric print cotton fabric
Plain cotton fabric
Lining fabric
Leather bag handles (2)
Matching sewing thread
items.techniques:
Bag Construction
Pocket Construction
Topstitching
Lining Construction
Boxed Corners
Slip Stitching
Hand Sewing
Handle Attachment
Turning and Pressing

steps.title: Floral Tote Bag - Create the Main Outer Panels
steps.content: Download and print the template. Cut one lower panel from geometric print fabric and one upper panel from floral fabric for each side of the tote. Cut a plain cotton strip measuring 1.18 x 14.96 inches and fold it in half lengthwise. Place the folded strip between the upper and lower panels with right sides together and stitch the centre seam. Press the folded strip toward the lower panel and topstitch to secure. Repeat for the second bag side.

tip.text: Press the seam thoroughly before topstitching to create a crisp decorative accent between the fabric sections.

steps.title: Floral Tote Bag - Make and Attach the Front Pockets
steps.content: Cut a floral square measuring 5.91 x 5.91 inches and a plain cotton rectangle measuring 5.91 x 6.30 inches. Stitch them together along one side, then fold and sew around the remaining edges, leaving a 2.36 inches turning opening. Turn right side out, press and topstitch along the decorative upper edge. Position the completed pocket centrally on the lower panel, approximately 0.39 inches below the folded accent strip, and stitch in place. Repeat for the opposite side.

tip.text: Uneven pocket placement will be highly visible on the finished tote, so measure carefully before stitching.

steps.title: Floral Tote Bag - Cut and Attach the Lining
steps.content: Lay the completed outer panels on the lining fabric and cut matching lining pieces. Place each lining piece right sides together with its corresponding outer panel and stitch across the top edge only.

tip.text: Match the lining and outer fabric edges precisely to keep the top opening smooth after turning.

steps.title: Floral Tote Bag - Sew the Bag Body
steps.content: Open the joined sections and place the outer fabrics right sides together and the lining fabrics right sides together. Stitch around the sides and base, leaving a 3.94 inches opening in the lining base for turning.

tip.text: Do not forget the turning gap in the lining or the tote cannot be turned right side out later.

steps.title: Floral Tote Bag - Create the Boxed Corners
steps.content: Flatten each lower corner by matching the side seam with the base seam. Stitch across each corner approximately 1.57 inches from the tip to create a boxed shape. Fold the corner tabs toward the base and tack them in place. Repeat for both the outer bag and the lining.

tip.text: Uneven corner measurements can result in a lopsided base that prevents the bag from standing properly.

steps.title: Floral Tote Bag - Turn and Close the Lining
steps.content: Turn the tote right side out through the opening in the lining. Fold the raw edges inward and close the opening with slip stitches. Push the lining inside the tote and smooth all seams into position.

tip.text: Use a point turner or blunt tool to gently shape the corners without stressing the stitching.

steps.title: Floral Tote Bag - Finish the Top Edge
steps.content: Press the upper edge of the tote carefully and topstitch around the entire opening approximately 0.20 inches from the edge to secure the lining and create a clean finish.

tip.text: Keep the lining rolled slightly toward the inside while topstitching to prevent it from showing on the exterior.

steps.title: Floral Tote Bag - Attach the Leather Handles
steps.content: Position the leather handles evenly on both sides of the tote. Hand sew them securely using matching thread and multiple passes of stitching to reinforce the attachment points. Check that both handles are aligned before completing the final stitches.

tip.text: Misaligned handles will cause the tote to hang unevenly when carried.

items.name: Everyday Slouch Shoulder Bag
items.materialNeeded:
Cotton fabric
Lining fabric
Coordinating sewing thread
items.techniques:
Bag Construction
Lining Construction
Topstitching
Curve Sewing
Slip Stitching
Turning and Pressing
Box Base Construction

steps.title: Slouchy Shopper Bag - Join the Strap Sections
steps.content: Download and print the template. Cut four outer fabric pieces and four lining pieces according to the pattern. Pair matching fabric pieces with right sides together and sew across the top of the strap section to create two hourglass-shaped outer pieces and two lining pieces. Press the seam allowances open and topstitch close to the seam for additional strength.

tip.text: Topstitching the strap seam helps reinforce an area that will carry the weight of the finished bag.

steps.title: Slouchy Shopper Bag - Construct the Strap Channels
steps.content: Place one outer piece and one lining piece right sides together. Sew along the curved edges on both sides of the strap section. Repeat for the second pair. Clip the curved seam allowances, turn both units right side out and press. Pass one side of each hourglass-shaped section through the opposite strap channel to create the interlocking bag structure.

tip.text: Clip curved seam allowances carefully without cutting into the stitching to help the strap channels turn smoothly.

steps.title: Slouchy Shopper Bag - Assemble the Main Bag Body
steps.content: Match the outer fabrics and lining fabrics on one side of the first hourglass unit with right sides together. Stitch in one continuous seam from the outer fabric through the lining. Repeat on the opposite unit. Join both completed sections together in the same way to create the full slouchy bag shape.

tip.text: Twisting one of the strap sections during assembly can prevent the bag from hanging correctly once completed.

steps.title: Slouchy Shopper Bag - Attach the Outer Base Panel
steps.content: Cut a 9.45 x 9.45 inches square from outer fabric. Turn the bag inside out and align the corners of the square with the centre points of the bag body beneath each strap. Pin carefully and stitch the square to the outer fabric bottom edges to form the bag base. Turn the bag right side out and fold the lining over the outer bag.

tip.text: Accurate corner placement is essential because an off-centre base panel can distort the overall shape of the bag.

steps.title: Slouchy Shopper Bag - Add the Lining Base and Finish
steps.content: Cut a matching 9.45 x 9.45 inches square from lining fabric. Sew it to the lining base edges in the same way, leaving a 3.94 inches turning gap along one side. Turn the bag right side out through the opening, fold the raw edges inward and close the gap with slip stitches. Push the lining into the bag, press thoroughly and topstitch around both straps to complete the project.

tip.text: Press the straps flat before the final topstitching to achieve a cleaner and more professional finish.

items.name: Reversible Tie-Handle Market Tote
items.materialNeeded:
Main fabric design A
Main fabric design B
Coordinating sewing thread
items.techniques:
Bag Construction
Reversible Bag Construction
Curve Sewing
Boxed Corners
Topstitching
Ladder Stitch
Turning and Pressing
Pattern Cutting

steps.title: Tied Tote Bag - Cut Outer and Lining Pieces
steps.content: Download and print the pattern. Fold one fabric piece in half lengthwise and place the template with the straight edge aligned to the fold. Cut around the template and unfold to create one full-size bag piece. Repeat to cut two outer bag pieces and two lining pieces.

tip.text: Keep the template aligned precisely on the fold to ensure both sides of the bag remain symmetrical.

steps.title: Tied Tote Bag - Sew the Outer and Lining Bags
steps.content: Place the two outer pieces right sides together and stitch from the top of one handle, down the side seam, across the bottom edge and up the opposite side using a 0.39 inches seam allowance. Trim the lower corners and press the seams open. Repeat with the lining pieces, leaving a 3.15 inches opening in the centre of the bottom seam for turning.

tip.text: Do not forget to leave the turning gap in the lining or the bag cannot be turned right side out later.

steps.title: Tied Tote Bag - Form the Outer Bag Boxed Corners
steps.content: With the outer bag still inside out, flatten one bottom corner so the side seam aligns directly with the bottom seam. Measure 1.97 inches from the tip of the corner and draw a line perpendicular to the seam. Stitch across the line and repeat on the second corner.

tip.text: If the side seam and bottom seam are not perfectly aligned, the boxed corners may become uneven and affect the bag shape.

steps.title: Tied Tote Bag - Secure the Outer Corner Folds
steps.content: Fold each stitched corner toward the base of the bag along the seam line. Hand sew the corner tip to the bottom seam allowance to hold it flat and reduce bulk inside the bag.

tip.text: Tacking the folded corner in place helps the bag maintain a cleaner structure during use.

steps.title: Tied Tote Bag - Create the Lining Boxed Corners
steps.content: Repeat the boxed corner process on both bottom corners of the lining. Flatten each corner, stitch across the marked line and secure the folded corner to the lining seam allowance with hand stitching.

tip.text: Using different corner measurements on the lining and outer bag can cause the lining to fit poorly inside the finished tote.

steps.title: Tied Tote Bag - Join the Outer Bag and Lining
steps.content: Turn the outer bag right side out and place it inside the lining so the right sides are facing each other. Match the side seams and handle edges carefully, then pin around the entire opening. Stitch all the way around the upper edge using a 0.39 inches seam allowance.

tip.text: Take extra care around the curved handle sections to prevent puckers or uneven seam allowances.

steps.title: Tied Tote Bag - Clip Curves and Turn the Bag
steps.content: Clip small notches into the curved seam allowances around the handles without cutting through the stitching. Turn the entire bag right side out through the opening left in the lining. Carefully shape the handles and press all seams flat.

tip.text: Clip only into the seam allowance. Cutting into the seam line can weaken the handle area.

steps.title: Tied Tote Bag - Close the Lining Opening
steps.content: Fold the raw edges of the turning opening inward and close the gap neatly using ladder stitch. Push the lining fully inside the tote and smooth the lining into position.

tip.text: Press the ladder-stitched opening after closing it to help the repair blend into the surrounding seam.

steps.title: Tied Tote Bag - Finish and Tie the Handles
steps.content: Press the entire tote thoroughly, paying special attention to the handle curves and upper edges. Tie a knot in each handle to create the finished slouchy tote shape and adjust the knot position to achieve the desired handle length.

tip.text: Tie both handles at matching heights to keep the bag balanced and comfortable when carried.

items.name: Everyday Reversible Shoulder Tote
items.materialNeeded:
Printed fabric, 23.62 x 35.43 inches
Plain fabric, 23.62 x 35.43 inches
Elastic, 3.94 inches
Shank button, 0.79 inches
Coordinating sewing thread
items.techniques:
Bag Construction
Reversible Bag Construction
Pocket Construction
Topstitching
Curve Sewing
Ladder Stitch
Turning and Pressing
Elastic Loop Closure

steps.title: Reversible Tote Bag - Cut Fabric Pieces and Prepare Pocket
steps.content: Download and print the templates. Cut two outer bag pieces from the printed fabric and two lining pieces from the plain fabric. Using the fabric offcut from between the handles, cut a pocket piece measuring 5.91 inches long. Press the curved pocket edges inward. Fold the top edge under by 0.39 inches and then again by 1.18 inches, press well and stitch the hem. Position the pocket on one lining piece approximately 1.97 inches below the top edge and stitch around the sides and bottom, leaving the top edge open.

tip.text: Press the pocket folds before stitching to achieve a cleaner edge and more accurate pocket placement.

steps.title: Reversible Tote Bag - Add the Elastic Closure Loop
steps.content: Fold the elastic piece in half to form a loop. Place it at the centre top edge of the second lining piece with the raw ends aligned to the fabric edge. Stitch close to the edge to secure the elastic loop in place.

tip.text: Ensure the loop faces inward toward the fabric before stitching so it turns out correctly after assembly.

steps.title: Reversible Tote Bag - Sew the Outer Bag Panels
steps.content: Place the two printed outer bag pieces right sides together. Pin around the side and bottom edges and stitch using a 0.24 inches seam allowance. Press the seam allowances open to reduce bulk and improve the bag shape.

tip.text: Skipping the pressing step can leave bulky seams that prevent the reversible bag from sitting neatly.

steps.title: Reversible Tote Bag - Construct the Lining Bag
steps.content: Place the two lining pieces right sides together and stitch around the side and bottom edges using the same seam allowance. Press the seams open and turn the lining right side out.

tip.text: Keep the seam allowance consistent on both the lining and outer bag to ensure they fit together smoothly.

steps.title: Reversible Tote Bag - Join the Handles
steps.content: Insert the right-side-out lining into the outer bag so the right sides are facing each other. Match the side seams carefully. Pin around each of the four handle openings and stitch approximately 0.24 inches from the edge, leaving the short top ends of the handles open.

tip.text: Double-check seam alignment before stitching because mismatched handles will be noticeable on the finished reversible bag.

steps.title: Reversible Tote Bag - Turn the Bag Right Side Out
steps.content: Press a 0.79 inches turning allowance inside the top edge of one handle on both the outer bag and lining. Turn the entire bag right side out by pulling all the fabric through one of the open handles. Carefully shape the remaining handles and press all seams flat.

tip.text: Pulling the fabric too aggressively through the handle opening can distort the curved handle shape.

steps.title: Reversible Tote Bag - Close the Handle Openings
steps.content: Insert the raw-ended handles into the prepared folded handle openings by approximately 0.79 inches. Baste the layers together, then machine stitch through all layers to secure. Add a line of topstitching if desired for additional reinforcement.

tip.text: Basting before machine stitching helps keep the handle layers aligned and prevents shifting.

steps.title: Reversible Tote Bag - Attach the Button
steps.content: Sew a shank button to the exterior side of the bag, positioning it approximately 4.72 inches below the upper edge on the same side as the elastic loop.

tip.text: Use several passes of strong thread when attaching the button because it will be used repeatedly to secure the folded bag.

steps.title: Reversible Tote Bag - Fold and Store the Tote
steps.content: To store the tote, fold both sides inward, fold the handles toward the centre and roll the bag tightly from the bottom upward. Stretch the elastic loop around the button to keep the tote neatly folded and compact.

tip.text: Roll the tote tightly and evenly to reduce bulk and make it easier to fit inside a handbag.

items.name: Little Cottage Appliqué Tote
items.materialNeeded:
Main cotton fabric for bag body
Contrast cotton fabric for roof
Printed cotton fabric for door
Printed cotton fabric for window
Cotton fabric scraps for applique details
Fusible web or applique adhesive
Pom pom trim
Cotton webbing or fabric straps
Lightweight interfacing
Embroidery floss
Matching sewing thread
items.techniques:
Applique
Topstitching
Bag Construction
Handle Attachment
Decorative Embroidery
Fusible Applique
Turning and Pressing
Edge Stitching

steps.title: Doll's House Bag - Cut the Main Bag Pieces
steps.content: Download and print the templates. Cut two outer bag pieces from the main fabric using the house-shaped template. Cut matching lining pieces if desired and apply lightweight interfacing to the outer fabric for additional structure.

tip.text: Fuse interfacing before trimming curved areas to help the bag maintain a crisp house shape.

steps.title: Doll's House Bag - Prepare the Decorative Applique Pieces
steps.content: Cut the roof, window, door and flower stem pieces from contrasting fabrics using the template shapes. Apply fusible web to the reverse side of the applique fabrics before cutting for easier placement.

tip.text: Label small applique pieces after cutting to avoid mixing up similar shapes during assembly.

steps.title: Doll's House Bag - Create the House Front Design
steps.content: Arrange the roof, window, door and stem pieces on one outer bag panel following the template layout. Fuse or pin the pieces in place and topstitch around each applique shape to secure them permanently.

tip.text: Applique pieces placed too close to the outer edge may be partially caught in later seam allowances.

steps.title: Doll's House Bag - Add Decorative Embellishments
steps.content: Embroider decorative details around the flowers, door and window using embroidery floss. Attach small buttons, beads or stitched accents if desired to enhance the doll's house appearance.

tip.text: Complete all embroidery before constructing the bag to keep stitching neat and accessible.

steps.title: Doll's House Bag - Attach the Pom Pom Trim
steps.content: Position pom pom trim along the lower edge of the roof section and baste it in place. Stitch carefully so the trim remains visible on the finished bag front.

tip.text: Keep the pom poms clear of the needle path while stitching to prevent broken needles.

steps.title: Doll's House Bag - Prepare and Position the Handles
steps.content: Cut or prepare two matching handles. Position them using the handle placement markings on the template and baste them securely to the front and back bag panels.

tip.text: Uneven handle placement can cause the bag to hang crooked when carried.

steps.title: Doll's House Bag - Assemble the Outer Bag
steps.content: Place the front and back outer bag pieces right sides together. Ensure the handles are tucked inside the bag and stitch around the sides and bottom edge, leaving the top opening unsewn.

tip.text: Check that the handles are folded away from the seam line before sewing the bag closed.

steps.title: Doll's House Bag - Construct and Insert the Lining
steps.content: Sew the lining pieces together in the same way as the outer bag. Place the outer bag inside the lining with right sides together and stitch around the top opening.

tip.text: Match the side seams of the lining and outer bag carefully to create a professional finish.

steps.title: Doll's House Bag - Turn and Finish the Bag
steps.content: Turn the bag right side out through the lining opening. Close the opening with hand stitching, push the lining inside the bag and press thoroughly. Topstitch around the upper edge if desired to complete the project.

tip.text: Shape the roof corners carefully after turning to preserve the house silhouette.

items.name: Pom-Pom Trim Makeup Pouch
items.finishedSize: Approximately 10.6 inches x 4.7 inches x 2.8 inches
items.materialNeeded:
Main cotton print fabric (2 coordinating prints)
Plain cotton lining fabric
Fusible interfacing
10-inch zipper
Leather belt
Ric-rac trim
Pom-pom trim
Decorative studs
Matching sewing thread
items.techniques: Pattern Cutting, Fusible Interfacing Application, Dart Sewing, Zipper Installation, Curved Seam Construction, Bag Assembly, Turning, Slip Stitching, Strap Loop Construction, Decorative Trim Application

steps.title: Cut and Prepare the Fabric
steps.content: Download and cut out the pouch template. Cut one complete outer piece and one flap piece from coordinating print fabrics. Cut two lining pieces and one lining flap from plain cotton fabric. Fuse interfacing to the wrong side of all lining pieces.

tip.text: Apply interfacing before cutting detailed curves whenever possible to help stabilize the fabric.

steps.title: Prepare the Zipper Ends
steps.content: Cut small fabric tabs and sew them over both ends of the zipper to create neat zipper stops and improve the finished appearance.

tip.text: Do not sew too close to the zipper pull, or it may become difficult to open and close the zipper.

steps.title: Sew the Bottom Tucks
steps.content: Mark the tuck placement on the lower edge of the outer pouch pieces. Fold and stitch the tucks as indicated on the pattern to create depth in the pouch.

tip.text: Press each tuck after stitching to keep the pouch shape symmetrical.

steps.title: Attach the Zipper to the Flap
steps.content: Place the zipper face down along the straight edge of the flap section. Position one lining flap piece on top with right sides together and sew close to the zipper teeth using a zipper foot.

tip.text: Keep the zipper teeth aligned evenly along the edge to prevent puckering.

steps.title: Attach the Main Body to the Zipper
steps.content: Align the opposite zipper tape with the upper edge of the outer pouch section. Place the remaining lining piece over the zipper and stitch close to the zipper teeth.

tip.text: Make sure the zipper is not twisted before sewing the second side.

steps.title: Press and Open the Zipper Assembly
steps.content: Open all fabric layers and press them away from the zipper. Leave the zipper partially open for later turning.

tip.text: Topstitching beside the zipper can help keep the fabric from catching in the zipper teeth.

steps.title: Join the Flap and Outer Body
steps.content: Place the completed outer pouch section and flap section right sides together. Stitch around the curved edge connecting the flap to the body.

tip.text: Clip curved seam allowances carefully without cutting through the stitching line.

steps.title: Turn the Flap Right Side Out
steps.content: Trim the seam allowance around the curved edge and turn the flap right side out. Shape the curve neatly and press flat.

tip.text: Use a blunt point turner to smooth the curved edge without damaging the fabric.

steps.title: Assemble the Outer Pouch
steps.content: Align the remaining edges of the outer pouch pieces, matching the tucks carefully. Sew around the outer pouch body.

tip.text: Misaligned tucks can make the pouch appear uneven once completed.

steps.title: Sew the Lining
steps.content: Match the lining pieces right sides together and sew around the edges, leaving an approximately 3-inch opening in the bottom seam for turning.

tip.text: Do not forget the turning opening or the pouch cannot be turned right side out later.

steps.title: Turn the Pouch Right Side Out
steps.content: Pull the entire pouch through the opening in the lining. Carefully shape all corners and curves.

tip.text: Open the zipper before turning to make the process much easier.

steps.title: Close the Lining Opening
steps.content: Fold the seam allowances inward and close the opening using a slip stitch for an invisible finish.

tip.text: Use matching thread color to help the hand stitching blend into the lining.

steps.title: Create the Belt Loops
steps.content: Cut two fabric rectangles measuring 2.8 inches x 3.1 inches. Fold each piece lengthwise with right sides together and stitch to form tubes. Turn right side out, fold 0.4 inch under at both ends, and press flat.

tip.text: Press the loops thoroughly before attaching them for a cleaner finish.

steps.title: Attach the Belt Loops
steps.content: Position the belt loops on the back of the pouch and stitch securely through all layers.

tip.text: Reinforce the ends of the loops with backstitching since they will carry the weight of the pouch.

steps.title: Add Decorative Trims
steps.content: Decorate the pouch with ric-rac trim, pom-pom braid, and decorative studs as desired. Secure all embellishments firmly to complete the project.

tip.text: Avoid placing studs too close to seams, as they may interfere with turning or future stitching.

items.name: Felt Easter Chicks
items.finishedSize: Approximately 2.4 inches tall x 2.4 inches wide
items.materialNeeded:
Yellow felt
Orange felt
White felt
Black felt
Ribbon (optional)
Gingham fabric (optional)
Striped jersey fabric
White pom-pom
Gold faux leather
Orange cord
Toy stuffing
Fabric glue
Matching sewing thread
items.techniques: Hand Sewing, Machine Sewing, Edge Stitching, Appliqué, Slip Stitching, Stuffing, Decorative Embellishment, Fabric Gluing

steps.title: Cut Out the Felt Pieces
steps.content: Using the templates, cut one front chick piece, one pair of back chick pieces, and one pair of wings from yellow felt. Cut one beak, one pair of heart-shaped feet, and one head feather piece from orange felt.

tip.text: Use sharp embroidery scissors to keep the curved wing and feather edges smooth and accurate.

steps.title: Prepare the Facial Features
steps.content: Cut two eye shapes from white felt and two small pupil circles from black felt. Cut one small bag piece from gold faux leather and a 2.4 inches x 4.7 inches rectangle from striped jersey fabric for the bobble hat.

tip.text: Make sure both eyes are mirrored and evenly sized before attaching them to avoid an uneven expression.

steps.title: Sew the Back Pieces
steps.content: Place the back chick pieces right sides together and stitch from the notch at the top down to the base. Attach the wings and head feather to the marked positions on the body.

tip.text: Keep the seam allowance consistent at 0.12 inches (3 mm) to ensure the front and back pieces align correctly later.

steps.title: Join the Front and Back
steps.content: Center the front chick piece over the back section. Stitch from the top seam down to the side notch, pivot around the curves, and continue to the base notch. Repeat on the opposite side, leaving the lower back opening unstitched.

tip.text: Clip tight curves before turning to help the rounded chick shape sit smoothly.

steps.title: Turn and Stuff the Chick
steps.content: Turn the chick right side out and gently shape the seams. Fill the body firmly with toy stuffing, paying special attention to the corners and lower body. Close the opening with hand slip stitches.

tip.text: Underfilling the body can make the chick collapse and lose its rounded shape.

steps.title: Attach the Feet and Face
steps.content: Glue or stitch the feet to the base of the chick. Secure the white eye pieces to the face, then fold the beak in half, glue along the fold, and stitch or glue it beneath the eyes.

tip.text: Allow any fabric glue to dry fully before handling to prevent pieces from shifting out of position.

steps.title: Make the Mini Bag
steps.content: Fold and stitch the faux leather bag following the template markings. Turn it right side out. Tie knots at both ends of a 9.5 inches length of orange cord and secure the cord beneath the bag flap.

tip.text: Use a leather needle or slightly longer stitch length when sewing faux leather to avoid perforating the material.

steps.title: Create the Bobble Hat
steps.content: Fold the jersey rectangle by 0.4 inches along one long edge, then fold again and stitch. Join the short ends together to form a tube and stitch the remaining edge closed.

tip.text: Stretching the jersey while sewing can distort the hat shape and make it fit poorly.

steps.title: Finish the Hat
steps.content: Gather the open end of the hat and secure it with stitches. Attach a small white pom-pom to the top.

tip.text: Use several passes of thread through the pom-pom to keep it securely attached during handling.

steps.title: Add Final Decorations
steps.content: Place the hat over the head feathers and secure it with a few hand stitches. If making the female chick version, tie a gingham bow and attach it in front of the head feathers.

tip.text: If the decoration is intended for young children, securely stitch all embellishments instead of relying solely on glue.

items.name: Lamb Doll
items.materialNeeded:
Assorted fabrics, cotton: plain; printed.
Felt, black.
Embroidery thread.
Fibre filling.
Lightweight quilt wadding.
Narrow elastic.
Pom-poms, white.
items.techniques: Stuffing, Embroidery, Garment Sewing, Gusset Sewing, Elastic Casing, Topstitching

steps.title: Cut the Body Pieces
steps.content: Cut the body, arm, and leg pieces from plain fabric. Cut the hands and feet from contrast fabric. Transfer all pattern markings before removing the templates.

tip.text: Missing seam matching marks can cause the arms and legs to be attached unevenly later.

steps.title: Sew the Arms and Legs
steps.content: Attach the hand pieces to the arms and the foot pieces to the legs. Sew each limb together, leaving the upper edge open and a turning gap along the back edge. Turn right side out, clip curves, and press.

tip.text: Clip curved seam allowances carefully without cutting through the stitching line.

steps.title: Attach Limbs to the Body
steps.content: Arrange the arms and legs around one body piece. Position the leg seams along the center and tack all limbs in place, matching the raw edges.

tip.text: Measure the distance from each limb to the body edge before tacking to keep both sides symmetrical.

steps.title: Construct and Stuff the Body
steps.content: Place the second body piece on top with right sides together and sew around the body, leaving a turning gap. Turn right side out and stuff firmly with fibre filling.

tip.text: Pack extra filling into the neck area because it will support the weight of the finished head.

steps.title: Stuff and Close the Limbs
steps.content: Stuff the arms and legs to a medium firmness through the turning gaps and close the openings with oversew stitches.

tip.text: Overstuffing the narrow legs can make them stiff and difficult to position naturally.

steps.title: Make the Ears
steps.content: Layer each ear piece with lightweight quilt wadding and sew around the edges, leaving the straight edge open. Clip curves, turn right side out, and press flat.

tip.text: Trim excess wadding close to the seam allowance before turning to reduce bulk.

steps.title: Construct the Face
steps.content: Sew the contrasting muzzle pieces to the front face. Position and tack the ears to the side edges of the head sections before assembling the remaining face seams.

tip.text: Uneven muzzle seams are very noticeable, so pin carefully along the curves before stitching.

steps.title: Insert the Head Gusset
steps.content: Sew the darts in the head gusset and attach it to the back of the head, matching the center seam and securing the ears within the seam.

tip.text: If the gusset center line does not match the head seam exactly, the finished head may twist.

steps.title: Stuff and Attach the Head
steps.content: Turn the head right side out and stuff firmly. Shape the muzzle and crown while filling. Place the head over the neck spike and hand-sew securely to the body.

tip.text: Add filling gradually around the nose area first to preserve the lamb's rounded facial shape.

steps.title: Embroider the Facial Features
steps.content: Mark the eye positions and embroider the eyes using black embroidery thread. Stitch the nose and add the embroidered mouth.

tip.text: Always mark both eyes before stitching, as correcting embroidered eyes afterward is difficult.

steps.title: Decorate the Head
steps.content: Attach the decorative pom-poms to the top of the head and make any final shaping adjustments.

tip.text: Sew pom-poms through the fabric and stuffing rather than only the surface fabric for better durability.

steps.title: Sew the Blouse
steps.content: Construct the blouse by sewing the shoulder seams, sleeves, collar, side seams, and hems. Add the front closure using snaps or decorative buttons.

tip.text: Clip the collar curves before turning to help the collar sit flat against the neckline.

steps.title: Make the Shorts
steps.content: Sew the pocket, assemble the shorts, attach the cuffs, and create the elastic casing at the waist. Thread the elastic through the casing and close the opening.

tip.text: Do not twist the elastic while threading it through the casing or the waistband will sit unevenly.

steps.title: Dress and Finish
steps.content: Dress the lamb in the blouse and shorts, fasten all closures, and make final adjustments to the outfit and body shaping.

tip.text: Give the finished garments a final press before dressing the doll for a cleaner professional appearance.

items.name: Pig Plush
items.techniques: Stuffing, Plush Sewing, Hand Sewing, Safety Eye Installation, Curve Sewing
items.materialNeeded:
Assorted fabrics, cotton: plain; printed.
Felt, black.
Embroidery thread.
Fibre filling.
Lightweight quilt wadding.
Narrow elastic.
Pom-poms, white.

steps.title: Cut the Plush Pieces
steps.content: Print the templates and cut one pair of head pieces, two pairs of ears, two pairs of arms, one pair of body and leg pieces, one nose piece, and one nose edge piece from the plush fabric. Mark the eye positions on the head pieces.

tip.text: Plush fabric has a directional nap. Make sure all pattern pieces are cut in the same direction to keep the finished toy looking consistent.

steps.title: Install the Safety Eyes
steps.content: Create small holes at the marked eye positions. Insert the safety eyes through the right side of the fabric and secure them with the supplied washers on the wrong side.

tip.text: Check the eye spacing before attaching the washers because safety eyes are difficult to remove once locked in place.

steps.title: Make the Ears
steps.content: Pair the ear pieces right sides together and sew around the curved edges, leaving the base open. Clip the seam allowance around the curves, turn right side out, and press.

tip.text: Clip closely around the ear curves to help the ears turn smoothly without puckering.

steps.title: Attach the Ears
steps.content: Position the ears on one head piece according to the template markings and stitch them in place.

tip.text: Uneven ear placement is very noticeable on animal toys, so check both ears are positioned symmetrically before sewing.

steps.title: Construct the Head
steps.content: Place the second head piece over the first with right sides together. Stitch around the head, sandwiching the ears inside the seam. Leave the neck opening, turn right side out, and stuff firmly.

tip.text: Avoid understuffing the cheeks and forehead, or the head may lose its rounded pig shape.

steps.title: Make the Snout
steps.content: Mark the nostrils and create small openings. Sew the short ends of the nose edge together to form a ring. Attach the ring to the snout piece and then sew the snout to the nose panel.

tip.text: Take extra care when sewing the circular snout seam, as uneven stitching can distort the shape of the nose.

steps.title: Stuff the Snout
steps.content: Fold the raw edge of the nose edge to the inside and hand stitch it in place. Add a small amount of stuffing to the snout to create dimension.

tip.text: Use only a small amount of filling in the snout to keep the nostril area soft and rounded.

steps.title: Sew the Arms
steps.content: Pair the arm pieces right sides together and sew around the curved edges, leaving the straight edge open. Turn right side out, stuff firmly, and close the opening.

tip.text: Large clumps of stuffing can create lumpy arms, so add filling gradually.

steps.title: Assemble the Body
steps.content: Position the completed arms on one body piece and sew in place. Layer the second body piece on top, stitch around the body and legs, leaving an opening for turning.

tip.text: Keep the arms tucked fully inside the body before stitching to avoid catching them in unwanted seams.

steps.title: Stuff the Body
steps.content: Turn the body right side out and stuff the body and legs firmly. Shape the legs evenly and close the opening with hand stitching.

tip.text: Use a knitting needle or stuffing tool to push filling all the way into the ends of the legs.

steps.title: Attach the Head
steps.content: Turn the neck seam allowance of the head inward and place the head over the stuffed neck. Hand stitch securely around the neckline and add extra filling if required.

tip.text: Ensure the neck is firmly stuffed before attaching the head or the head may droop forward.

steps.title: Finish the Pig
steps.content: Attach the completed snout to the face with hand slip stitches. Tie a pink ribbon around the neck and apply blush to the ears and cheeks if desired.

tip.text: Pin the snout in place and check it from multiple angles before stitching permanently.

items.name: Weather Wheel
items.techniques: Applique, Free Motion Embroidery, Felt Sewing, Fusible Webbing, Topstitching
items.materialNeeded:
Felt, various colours.
Fusible webbing.
Firm iron-on interfacing.
Split pin.
0.39 inches seam allowance used throughout, unless otherwise stated.

steps.title: Cut the Wheel Base
steps.content: Download and print the templates. Cut one circle from blue felt and fuse firm iron-on interfacing to the back to add stiffness to the wheel.

tip.text: Make sure the interfacing is completely fused to the felt before stitching, otherwise the wheel may wrinkle or distort.

steps.title: Prepare the Weather Shapes
steps.content: Trace each weather element onto fusible webbing and roughly cut them out. Iron the webbing onto the appropriate felt colours, then cut out the shapes neatly.

tip.text: Cutting small felt shapes before applying fusible webbing can make them difficult to position accurately.

steps.title: Mark the Wheel Layout
steps.content: Trace the wheel template onto tracing paper and use it as a guide to mark the weather sections on the felt wheel.

tip.text: Use a removable fabric marker so any visible guide marks can be removed after stitching.

steps.title: Fuse the Weather Applique
steps.content: Arrange the rainbow, clouds, sun, raindrops, lightning bolt, and snow shapes within their designated sections. Remove the paper backing and fuse each piece permanently in place.

tip.text: Check the placement of every applique piece before ironing because fused felt is difficult to reposition.

steps.title: Stitch the Applique Pieces
steps.content: Carefully machine stitch around each felt shape to permanently secure the applique to the wheel.

tip.text: Sewing too close to the edge of felt applique can cause the edges to curl or stretch.

steps.title: Add Embroidered Weather Details
steps.content: Use free-motion embroidery or hand embroidery to add decorative details such as sun rays, snowflakes, and weather accents.

tip.text: Practice free-motion embroidery on a felt scrap first to achieve smoother curves and more consistent stitches.

steps.title: Finish the Main Wheel
steps.content: Place the completed wheel onto a larger piece of felt with wrong sides together. Stitch around the outer edge and sew the dividing section lines through all layers. Trim the circle neatly.

tip.text: Keep the felt layers flat while stitching the section lines to prevent puckering between layers.

steps.title: Create the Spinner Window
steps.content: Cut the window piece from felt and fuse interfacing to the back. Embroider the 'Today's Weather' wording onto the top section.

tip.text: Skipping the interfacing can cause the spinner to lose its shape after repeated use.

steps.title: Cut the Viewing Opening
steps.content: Sew around the outer edges of the spinner piece and carefully cut out the center opening to create the viewing window.

tip.text: Cut the center opening only after sewing the perimeter to prevent the felt from stretching out of shape.

steps.title: Attach the Spinner
steps.content: Create a small hole in the center of both the wheel and the spinner. Join the two pieces using a split pin.

tip.text: Leave a small amount of play in the split pin so the spinner rotates smoothly.

steps.title: Test and Finish
steps.content: Rotate the spinner through each weather section and make any final trimming, stitching, or alignment adjustments.

tip.text: Overtightening the split pin can make the weather wheel difficult for children to turn.

items.name: Stacking Ring Toy
items.techniques: Stuffing, Hand Sewing, Curve Sewing, Dart Sewing, Applique, Embroidery
items.materialNeeded:
Fabric, printed cottons.
Toy stuffing.
Lightweight quilt wadding.
Stiff card, 6 inches square.
Buttons.
Embroidery thread.
Pinking shears.
1/4 inch seam allowance used throughout unless otherwise stated.

steps.title: Cut the Ring Pieces
steps.content: Print and cut the templates. Trace the ring shapes onto fabric with right sides together. Stitch around the curved edges, leaving the straight ends open, then cut out the shapes leaving a 1/4 inch seam allowance.

tip.text: Clip or notch the curved seam allowance before turning to help the rings maintain a smooth circular shape.

steps.title: Turn and Stuff the Rings
steps.content: Fold the seam allowance at each opening to the inside and tack in place. Turn the rings right side out and stuff them firmly and evenly. Join the open ends and close the ring with small hand stitches.

tip.text: Uneven stuffing can create flat spots that prevent the rings from stacking neatly.

steps.title: Make the Base Cushion
steps.content: Cut two 6 inch fabric squares. Sew around three sides with right sides together, leaving one side open. Turn right side out and insert a 6 inch square of stiff card followed by a firm layer of stuffing.

tip.text: Keep the card flat inside the cushion while adding stuffing or the base may become distorted.

steps.title: Close the Base
steps.content: Fold the raw edges of the opening inward and hand stitch the gap closed to create a padded base.

tip.text: Use a ladder stitch to create an almost invisible closure along the opening.

steps.title: Sew the Cone
steps.content: Cut the cone piece from patterned fabric. Fold it in half with right sides together and sew the long seam. Fold the lower edge inward, turn right side out, and stuff firmly.

tip.text: Understuffing the cone can cause it to bend when the rings are stacked onto it.

steps.title: Attach the Cone to the Base
steps.content: Position the cone in the center of the padded base and hand stitch it securely in place. Add additional stuffing if needed before closing the final section.

tip.text: Make sure the cone is perfectly centered or the stacking rings may lean to one side.

steps.title: Construct the Dog Head
steps.content: Sew the darts in the head pieces. Place the head sections right sides together, stitch around the shape, leaving the neck opening, then clip curves and turn right side out.

tip.text: Press the dart seams flat before assembly to help create a smoother head shape.

steps.title: Stuff the Head
steps.content: Firmly stuff the head, working filling into the nose and crown while leaving the lower neck area partially open.

tip.text: Push stuffing firmly into the nose first or the muzzle may collapse after use.

steps.title: Create the Neck Opening
steps.content: Work a finger into the center of the stuffing to create a cavity. Gather the raw neck edge with a running stitch and pull gently to shape the opening.

tip.text: Test the fit over the cone before tightening the gathering stitches completely.

steps.title: Make the Ears
steps.content: Cut two pairs of ear pieces and layer them with lightweight quilt wadding. Sew around the edges leaving a small opening, turn right side out, and close the gap.

tip.text: Leaving excess wadding in the seam allowance can make the ears bulky and difficult to shape.

steps.title: Add Facial Features
steps.content: Attach the ears to the head. Sew buttons in place for the eyes and embroider a triangular nose using embroidery thread.

tip.text: For toys intended for very young children, replace button eyes with embroidered features for safety.

steps.title: Finish the Stacking Toy
steps.content: Stack the completed rings onto the cone and place the dog head on top to secure the rings and complete the toy.

tip.text: Arrange the rings from largest to smallest to help children learn size sequencing while playing.

items.name: Axolotl Pair
items.finishedSize:
Big Axolotl: approximately 12 inches long
Small Axolotl: approximately 8 inches long
items.techniques: Hand Sewing, Stuffing, Ladder Stitch, Whip Stitch, Backstitch, Embroidery, Curve Sewing, Applique
items.materialNeeded:
Cotton fabric, assorted shades of pink.
Embroidery thread, assorted shades.
Black beads: 5 mm, 7 mm.
Fibre filling.
Lightweight quilt wadding or felt scraps.
Pinking shears.

steps.title: Prepare the Body Parts
steps.content: Cut the body, limb, and frond pieces from fabric according to the template. Pair the pieces with right sides together. Place quilt wadding or felt inside each limb and frond pair, then stitch along the marked lines, leaving the straight edges open. Trim the seam allowances with pinking shears, turn right side out, and press.

tip.text: Clip or pink the curved seam allowances carefully to prevent bulky seams and distorted shapes after turning.

steps.title: Embroider the Fronds and Limbs
steps.content: Using embroidery thread, sew decorative ladder stitches across each frond. Add embroidered finger details to the ends of the limb pieces.

tip.text: Keep stitch spacing consistent on matching fronds so both sides of the axolotl appear balanced.

steps.title: Position the Features
steps.content: Lay one body piece flat and arrange the embroidered fronds and limbs around the edges in their correct positions. Fold the raw edges of each piece inward and pin securely to the body.

tip.text: Mixing up the orientation of the fronds can result in an uneven appearance once the body is assembled.

steps.title: Assemble the Body
steps.content: Place the second body piece on top with right sides together. Stitch around the axolotl, leaving a turning and stuffing gap between two limbs. Clip the curves and turn the body right side out, ensuring all limbs and fronds are fully pulled through.

tip.text: Check that no fronds or limbs are accidentally caught in the seam before completing the body stitching.

steps.title: Stuff the Axolotl
steps.content: Firmly stuff the body with fibre filling, paying special attention to the head, tail tip, and curved body sections to create an even shape.

tip.text: Add stuffing in small amounts and distribute it gradually to avoid lumps inside the finished toy.

steps.title: Close the Opening
steps.content: Fold the seam allowances inside the opening and close the gap with small oversew stitches or a neat ladder stitch.

tip.text: Pulling the closing stitches too tightly can create puckering along the body seam.

steps.title: Embroider the Smile
steps.content: Use two strands of embroidery thread to sew a curved backstitched smile. Work a whip stitch over the backstitching to smooth and define the curve.

tip.text: Mark the smile lightly before stitching to keep both ends positioned evenly.

steps.title: Attach the Eyes
steps.content: Sew the appropriate size black beads securely above each end of the smile to create the eyes.

tip.text: Reinforce bead attachments with multiple passes of thread, especially if the toy will be handled frequently.

items.name: Giraffe Plush
items.techniques: Stuffing, Hand Sewing, Curve Sewing, Dart Sewing, Applique, Slip Stitch, Topstitching
items.materialNeeded:
Printed cottons, two fat quarters.
Toy stuffing.
Quilt wadding.
Felt.
Small black buttons, two.
1/4 inch seam allowance used throughout unless otherwise stated.

steps.title: Cut the Fabric Pieces
steps.content: Print and cut out the templates. Trace all pattern pieces onto the wrong side of the fabric and cut them out, adding a 1/4 inch seam allowance. Cut the ear pieces from contrasting fabrics and prepare the felt circles for the eyes.

tip.text: Forgetting to add seam allowance when tracing can result in a finished giraffe that is smaller than intended.

steps.title: Make the Ossicones
steps.content: Place the ossicone pieces right sides together and sew around the curved edges, leaving the bottom edge open. Clip the curves, turn right side out, and stuff firmly, leaving approximately 1/2 inch unstuffed at the base. Tack across the opening to keep the stuffing in place.

tip.text: Use a knitting needle or stuffing tool to push filling firmly into the rounded tips so the ossicones stand upright.

steps.title: Create the Ears
steps.content: Place the ear pieces right sides together with quilt wadding layered inside. Sew around the edges, leaving the straight edge open. Clip curves, turn right side out, and press. Pleat the lower edge and tack it in place.

tip.text: Trim bulky seam allowances around the ear curves to help the ears lie smoothly after turning.

steps.title: Construct the Nose
steps.content: Sew the nose pieces together along the straight edges that attach to the head and body. Stitch the curved nose pieces right sides together, clip the curves, and turn the nose right side out.

tip.text: Insufficient clipping on tight curves can cause the nose to look wrinkled after turning.

steps.title: Attach Features to the Head
steps.content: Pin and tack the completed ossicones and ears onto the front head piece, aligning them with the pattern markings. Position and pin the nose section in place.

tip.text: Baste the ears and ossicones securely before assembly so they do not shift while sewing the body.

steps.title: Assemble the Giraffe Body
steps.content: Place the front and back body pieces right sides together and sew around the entire giraffe, leaving a 2 1/2 inch gap along a straight section for turning and stuffing.

tip.text: Check that the ears and ossicones are fully tucked inside the body before stitching to avoid catching them in the seam.

steps.title: Turn and Stuff the Toy
steps.content: Clip the curved seam allowances and turn the giraffe right side out. Firmly stuff the nose, neck, body, and legs with toy filling. Fold the raw edges of the opening inward and close the gap with slip stitches.

tip.text: Stuff the neck firmly to help the finished giraffe maintain its upright shape.

steps.title: Shape the Ossicones
steps.content: Remove the temporary tacking stitches from the ossicones and carefully manipulate the stuffing downward into the seam allowance so the ossicones stand upright.

tip.text: Leaving too much stuffing trapped at the tip can prevent the ossicones from standing neatly.

steps.title: Add Facial Features
steps.content: Cut two small felt circles and stitch them onto the face as eye patches. Sew the buttons securely in the center of each felt circle. Embroider or stitch the nostrils onto the nose.

tip.text: If the toy is intended for young children, replace button eyes with embroidered features for safety.

items.name: Guinea Pig Plush
items.finishedSize:
Large: 6.7 inches x 9.8 inches
Small: 3.1 inches x 5.1 inches
items.techniques: Patchwork Sewing, Hand Sewing, Slip Stitch, Stuffing, Embroidery, Curve Sewing, Gusset Insertion
items.materialNeeded:
Fabric, cotton: assorted prints, plain.
Felt, pale pink.
Black buttons, 5 mm and 12 mm.
Toy stuffing.
Embroidery thread, pink.
1/4 inch seam allowance used throughout unless otherwise stated.

steps.title: Create the Patchwork Body Panels
steps.content: Print and cut out the templates. Trace the head, middle, bottom, and gusset pieces onto cotton fabrics. Stitch the body sections together following the template markings, matching the curved seams carefully. Press the seams to one side.

tip.text: Stretching curved seams while sewing can distort the body shape and make the gusset difficult to attach later.

steps.title: Attach the Gusset
steps.content: Pin and tack the gusset to the lower edge of the body, starting at the marked points and working gradually around both sides. Remove the tacking stitches after sewing.

tip.text: Use plenty of pins around tight curves to help the gusset lie smoothly against the body panels.

steps.title: Assemble the Guinea Pig Body
steps.content: Pin the top of the head and back sections together. Stitch around the body, leaving a 2 inch opening in the center for turning and stuffing. Clip the curves and turn right side out.

tip.text: Clip curved seam allowances carefully without cutting into the stitching line to achieve smooth rounded contours.

steps.title: Stuff and Close the Body
steps.content: Firmly stuff the guinea pig with toy filling until evenly shaped. Fold the raw edges of the opening inward and close the gap using slip stitches.

tip.text: Add stuffing gradually in small amounts to avoid lumps and maintain a symmetrical body shape.

steps.title: Create the Decorative Patch
steps.content: Cut a decorative patch with seam allowance, fold the edges under, and slip-stitch it onto the guinea pig to cover the body opening.

tip.text: Unevenly folded seam allowances can make the patch appear bulky and uneven once attached.

steps.title: Make the Ears
steps.content: Cut one ear piece from felt and one from fabric for each ear. Place right sides together and sew around the curved edge. Turn right side out, fold under the lower raw edge, and slip-stitch the ears to the head.

tip.text: Trim excess bulk from the ear curves before turning to help the ears sit neatly against the head.

steps.title: Create the Feet
steps.content: Cut eight felt feet and pair them together. Sew around the edges with running stitches, adding a small amount of stuffing before closing.

tip.text: Use only a tiny amount of stuffing so the feet remain soft and proportional to the body.

steps.title: Attach the Feet
steps.content: Sew the completed feet securely to the underside of the guinea pig using several passes of strong thread.

tip.text: Reinforce the stitches well because the feet will experience the most handling during play.

steps.title: Add the Facial Features
steps.content: Sew black buttons onto each side of the head for the eyes. Use pink embroidery thread to stitch the nose and mouth details.

tip.text: For toys intended for very young children, replace button eyes with embroidered eyes for improved safety.

items.name: Felt Fruit Set
items.finishedSize:
Large Strawberry: 5 inches x 3 inches
Small Strawberry: 3.5 inches x 2 inches
Raspberry: 2 inches x 1.5 inches
Blueberry: 1 inch diameter
items.techniques: Machine Stitching, Hand Sewing, Embroidery, Running Stitch, Gathering Stitch, Needle Felting, Stuffing, Decorative Stitching, Slip Stitch
items.materialNeeded:
Felt, assorted colours.
Embroidery thread, assorted colours.
Embroidery thread, white.
Toy stuffing.
Felting needle.
Green felt for strawberry tops and raspberry leaves.
Dry felting wool.
Scrap paper for embroidery stabilizing.

steps.title: Prepare Strawberry Pieces
steps.content: Cut three strawberry body pieces from red felt, three leaf tops from green felt, and stem pieces using the templates. Transfer the seed markings onto the felt and machine stitch the seed details. Highlight the seeds with small white embroidery stitches.

tip.text: Place scrap paper beneath the felt while stitching the seed details to prevent the felt from stretching.

steps.title: Assemble and Stuff the Strawberries
steps.content: Fold each strawberry piece and sew the side seams together, leaving a small opening. Cut small slits around the top edge, sew a gathering stitch around the opening, turn right side out, and stuff firmly before pulling the gathering thread closed.

tip.text: Many beginners underfill the pointed tip, resulting in a flat-looking strawberry instead of a plump fruit shape.

steps.title: Create Leaf Tops and Stems
steps.content: Stitch leaf vein details onto the green felt tops. Cut a small hole in the center and use a dry felting needle to form firm stems. Insert each stem through the leaf top and hand sew securely.

tip.text: Use extra care when working with a felting needle, as the sharp barbed needle can easily puncture fingers.

steps.title: Finish the Strawberries
steps.content: Attach the completed leaf tops and stems to each stuffed strawberry. Hand stitch around the underside of the leaf tops to conceal the gathering stitches and create a neat finish.

tip.text: Use matching thread when sewing the leaf tops in place so the stitches disappear into the felt.

steps.title: Create Raspberry Panels
steps.content: Cut three raspberry sections from felt. Machine stitch the segment outlines onto each piece, then add a single white stitch to every segment to create texture and definition.

tip.text: Sew directly over the marked segment lines twice to create a more pronounced raspberry texture.

steps.title: Assemble the Raspberries
steps.content: Fold each raspberry section in half and sew the side seams. Turn right side out and carefully push out the bottom point. Add small cuts around the top edge and sew a running gathering stitch.

tip.text: Cutting the gathering tabs deeper than instructed can weaken the felt and cause tearing when tightening the thread.

steps.title: Stuff and Shape the Raspberries
steps.content: Partially tighten the gathering thread, add stuffing, then fully close the opening. Bring the thread through the center of the fruit to shape the raspberry and secure the stuffing firmly inside.

tip.text: Pull the shaping thread through the center of the raspberry to create a realistic dimpled fruit profile.

steps.title: Attach Raspberry Leaves
steps.content: Create a small leaf top for each raspberry and stitch it through the center. Pull the thread gently so the leaf sinks slightly into the fruit before fastening securely.

tip.text: Avoid pulling the thread too tightly, as this can distort the raspberry shape and compress the stuffing unevenly.

steps.title: Create the Blueberries
steps.content: Cut small purple felt rectangles and roll them tightly. Use a dry felting needle to compact the fibers repeatedly until round berry shapes form.

tip.text: Always work on a felting mat or foam surface to protect both your needle and work area.

steps.title: Add Blueberry Details
steps.content: Hand sew a small cross stitch in lighter purple thread at the top of each blueberry to create the characteristic blossom-end detail.

tip.text: Keep the cross stitch small and centered to maintain the scale and realism of the blueberry.

items.name: Patchwork Mouse Pair
items.techniques: Paper Piecing, Hand Appliqué, Running Stitch, Gathering Stitch, Machine Sewing, Hand Sewing, Edge Stitching, Stuffing, Slip Stitch, Decorative Embroidery
items.materialNeeded:
Spotty cotton fabric (yellow and turquoise)
Pink felt
Yellow felt
Black beads
Black embroidery thread
Toy stuffing
Polyester wadding
Small plastic bags
Sand or rice for weighting
Paper piecing templates (10 hexagons per mouse).

steps.title: Prepare the Hexagon Panels
steps.content: Print and cut the templates. Create ten 1.57 inches hexagons using the paper-piecing method. For each mouse, prepare five yellow hexagons and five turquoise hexagons.

tip.text: Keep all paper piecing templates in place until the hexagons are fully stitched to maintain accurate shapes.

steps.title: Cut the Mouse Body
steps.content: Fold the spotty fabric right sides together and cut the mouse body piece on the fold according to the template.

tip.text: Cutting without placing the body on the fold will result in a body piece that is too narrow.

steps.title: Attach the Hexagon Appliqué
steps.content: Arrange the nine decorative hexagons on the body fabric in two rows. Remove the paper templates and hand stitch or oversew the hexagons onto the fabric.

tip.text: Position the hexagons before stitching and take a photo as a reference to keep the layout symmetrical.

steps.title: Shape the Face
steps.content: Gather the center front of the body using two rows of running stitches. Pull the threads gently to create the distinctive mouse nose and face contour.

tip.text: Pull gathering threads gradually to avoid snapping the thread or puckering the fabric unevenly.

steps.title: Sew the Body Seam
steps.content: Fold the body right sides together and stitch the long curved back seam. Leave the bottom opening unstitched for turning and filling.

tip.text: Do not sew across the bottom opening or it will be difficult to add the weighting material later.

steps.title: Turn and Add Initial Stuffing
steps.content: Turn the mouse right side out. Firmly stuff the upper portion of the body with polyester wadding, shaping the head and shoulders as you work.

tip.text: Use a blunt turning tool or chopstick to push stuffing neatly into the nose area.

steps.title: Add Weight to the Base
steps.content: Fill a small plastic bag with sand or rice and place it inside the lower body. Add additional stuffing around the bag to stabilize the mouse and help it function as a bookend.

tip.text: Always seal the sand or rice inside a plastic bag to prevent moisture damage or leakage over time.

steps.title: Close the Bottom Opening
steps.content: Cut a felt circle to cover the base opening. Place it over the wadding and stitch securely around the circumference using oversewing stitches.

tip.text: Keep the felt circle slightly taut while stitching to create a flat, stable base.

steps.title: Make and Attach the Ears
steps.content: Cut two ear shapes from felt and sew them to the top of the mouse head. Position them evenly on both sides of the face.

tip.text: Uneven ear placement can make the mouse appear tilted, so check positioning before stitching permanently.

steps.title: Add Eyes and Tail
steps.content: Sew two black beads onto the face for eyes. Cut a long tapered strip of felt, curl it gently, and stitch it to the back of the mouse to create the tail.

tip.text: If the bookend will be used around small children, replace bead eyes with embroidered eyes for safety.

items.name: Easter Bunny
items.techniques: Template Tracing, Fabric Cutting, Machine Sewing, Curve Sewing, Turning and Stuffing, Pleat Formation, Tack Stitching, Slip Stitching, Hand Embroidery, Seam Clipping
items.materialNeeded:
Cotton fat quarters (main fabric and contrast fabric)
Lightweight 2 oz quilt wadding
Toy stuffing
Embroidery thread
Pinking shears.

steps.title: Cut and Prepare Ear Pieces
steps.content: Place the main and contrast fabrics right sides together. Using the ear template, trace and cut two ear shapes. Layer each ear with quilt wadding and stitch along the marked lines, leaving the straight base edge open.

tip.text: Keep the fabric and wadding layers smooth while stitching to prevent puckers along the ear edges.

steps.title: Shape the Ears
steps.content: Trim the ear pieces with pinking shears, leaving a 5mm seam allowance. Remove excess wadding from the ear bases, turn right side out, and press. Form a small pleat at the contrast side of each ear and secure with a tack stitch.

tip.text: Leaving too much wadding in the seam allowance can make the ears bulky and difficult to shape neatly.

steps.title: Create the Body Pieces
steps.content: Cut two egg-shaped body pieces from the main fabric. Place them right sides together and pin. Stitch a dart at the base as marked on the template.

tip.text: Accurate darts help the bunny stand upright and create a rounded body shape.

steps.title: Position and Attach the Ears
steps.content: Lay the prepared ears onto one body piece, matching the raw edges and positioning them according to the template markings. Pin securely in place.

tip.text: Double-check ear placement before sewing, as uneven ears will be very noticeable on the finished bunny.

steps.title: Assemble the Bunny Body
steps.content: Place the second body piece on top with right sides together. Stitch around the body, enclosing the ear bases within the seam. Leave a 1.57 inches opening at the base for turning and stuffing.

tip.text: Do not accidentally stitch across the turning gap or stuffing the bunny later will be difficult.

steps.title: Turn and Stuff
steps.content: Clip curves where necessary and turn the bunny right side out through the opening. Ensure the ears are fully turned and shaped. Firmly stuff the body with toy filling until evenly rounded.

tip.text: Use a blunt tool or chopstick to gently push stuffing into the top of the head and ear bases.

steps.title: Close the Base
steps.content: Fold the raw edges of the opening inward and close the gap neatly using slip stitches.

tip.text: Use matching thread and small slip stitches to create an almost invisible closure.

steps.title: Embroider the Face
steps.content: Using three strands of embroidery thread, hand embroider the eyes, nose, and mouth in the center of the upper front section of the bunny as shown in the pattern guide.

tip.text: Keep facial features centered and symmetrical to maintain the bunny's balanced appearance.

items.name: Platypus Plush
items.techniques: Template Tracing, Machine Sewing, Curve Sewing, Clipping Curves, Turning and Stuffing, Running Stitch, Hand Sewing, Overcasting, Edge Tacking, Button Attachment
items.materialNeeded:
Assorted cotton prints
Toy stuffing
Sewing thread
0.79 inches x 0.79 inches buttons
0.79 inches x 0.39 inches buttons.

steps.title: Cut and Sew the Upper Body
steps.content: Trace all template pieces onto the wrong side of the fabric and add a 5mm seam allowance. Cut out the pieces. Place the two upper body sections right sides together and stitch along the long curved edge. Clip the seam allowances around the curves.

tip.text: Make small, evenly spaced clips around tight curves so the body turns smoothly without puckering.

steps.title: Assemble and Stuff the Body
steps.content: Fold under 5mm along all straight edges of the top and bottom body sections, including the leg, head, and tail openings. Tack the folds in place. Position the top and bottom body pieces right sides together and stitch along all curved edges between the folded openings. Clip the curves, turn the body right side out through an opening, and fill firmly with toy stuffing. Pin the folded openings closed temporarily.

tip.text: Avoid overstuffing too early. Leave enough flexibility to position and attach the beak, feet, and tail neatly.

steps.title: Create the Tail and Beak
steps.content: Sew the tail and beak pieces right sides together along the curved edges, leaving the straight edge open. Clip the curves, turn right side out, and lightly stuff. Fold in the seam allowances and close the openings with small running stitches approximately 3mm from the edge.

tip.text: Use a blunt turning tool to push out the beak tip without damaging the fabric.

steps.title: Make the Feet
steps.content: Construct the feet using the same method as the tail. Sew around the toe shapes, leaving the straight edge open. Clip curves and corners, turn right side out, lightly stuff, and close the opening with hand stitching. Add rows of running stitches between the toes and gently pull the threads to create definition and curl the toes slightly.

tip.text: Do not pull the shaping stitches too tightly or the fabric may distort around the toes.

steps.title: Insert and Attach the Beak
steps.content: Open the folded beak gap in the body and insert the finished beak. Pin securely and overcast the opening closed using small hand stitches. Add extra stuffing to the body if needed before fully closing the seam.

tip.text: Check that the beak sits centered before stitching permanently, as repositioning afterward is difficult.

steps.title: Attach the Tail and Feet
steps.content: Position the tail at the rear opening and stitch securely in place. Attach the feet to the underside of the body using strong hand stitches through the folded openings.

tip.text: Use doubled thread when attaching the feet because they receive the most handling and stress.

steps.title: Add the Eyes
steps.content: Create two stacked button eyes using one 0.79 inches button and one 0.39 inches button for each eye. Sew the stacked buttons securely to either side of the head.

tip.text: If the toy is intended for a young child, embroider the eyes instead of using buttons to avoid a choking hazard.

items.name: Anteater Doll
items.finishedSize: Approximately 18 x 7 inches (including trunk and legs)
